title: A note on zero density results implying large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials

thm:1.2.summary:
Zero density results imply large value estimates: a bound $N(1-
u,T)\ll T^{A(
u)
u+o(1)}$ on the zeros of $\zeta$ yields a corresponding bound on $|\{t\in[-T,T]:|D(t)|\ge N^\sigma\}|$ for Dirichlet polynomials $D$ — the converse of the classical large-values ⟹ zero-density direction (Heath-Brown), so the two are essentially equivalent.

prop:1.1.summary: Partition the zeros $\mathcal{T}=\{\rho=\beta+i\gamma:\zeta(\rho)=0,\beta\ge1-\nu,|\gamma|\le T\}$ into $O(1)$ well-separated sub-classes. The combinatorial setup that lets a zero-density bound be turned into a large-value bound class by class.

conjecture:1.4.summary:
A stronger density hypothesis: for $
u\in[0,	frac12-\epsilon)$, $N(1-
u,T)\ll_\epsilon T^{(2-\delta)
u+\epsilon}$ for some $\delta=\delta(\epsilon)>0$ — i.e. the density hypothesis with a power saving. The hypothesis under which the strongest prime-in-short-intervals consequence below holds.

thm:1.5.summary: Conditional on conjecture:1.4: for $h=(\log X)^{2+\epsilon}$, the interval $(x,x+h]$ contains the expected number of primes for all but $o(X)$ of $x\le X$. Almost-all primes in nearly-shortest-possible intervals — the headline number-theoretic payoff.

lem:2.1.summary: Large values of zeta-sums: a bound on $|\{t\in[-T,T]:|\sum_{M<m\le M'}m^{-1-it}|\ge M^{-\nu}\}|$ in terms of zero-density data. Converts the size of a large-value set of a Dirichlet polynomial into the size of a large-value set of a $\zeta$-sum.

lem:2.2.summary: Dichotomy for the quantity $R_{\sigma,\eta}(T)$ (the measure of the set where a $\zeta$-sum is large): either it is $\le4T^{(1-\sigma)/2}$ or it is controlled by a zero-count. The case split driving the inductive argument.

lem:2.3.summary: A large value of $\zeta(s)$ implies a nearby zero: if $|\zeta(\sigma+it)|\ge T^{1/(\log\log T)^{10}}$ then $\zeta$ has a zero with real part $\ge\sigma'$ close to $\sigma$ and imaginary part near $t$. The bridge from large $\zeta$-values to the zero-counting function.

lem:2.4.summary: Measure bound for the set where $|\zeta(\sigma+it)|$ is large, in terms of a zero-count parameter — the quantitative form of the large-value/zero correspondence.

lem:2.5.summary: Existence of a good real-part parameter $\beta$ for the iteration: given the data, one can choose the line $\mathrm{Re}=\beta$ on which to compare $\zeta$-sums. A bookkeeping lemma for the descent.

lem:2.6.summary: Closing bound: $R_{\sigma,\eta}(T)\ll_{\epsilon,\eta}T^{2\nu+\cdots}$ — the final measure estimate for the large-$\zeta$-value set, obtained by iterating the dichotomy lem:2.2.

lem:3.1.summary: Approximate functional equation for $\zeta$: $\zeta(\sigma+it)=\sum_{n\le x}n^{-\sigma-it}+\chi(\sigma+it)\sum_{n\le y}n^{-1+\sigma+it}+\text{small}$ for $2\pi xy=|t|$. The standard device approximating $\zeta$ by short Dirichlet sums.

lem:3.2.summary: The convexity bound $|\zeta(\sigma+it)|\ll|t|^{(1-\sigma)/2}\log|t|$ for $\tfrac12\le\sigma\le1$. The trivial pointwise size of $\zeta$ in the critical strip, used to control the 'large but not too large' regime.

lem:3.3.summary: There are $\ll\log(|U|+2)$ zeros of $\zeta$ (with multiplicity) in $\{\mathrm{Re}(\rho)\ge0,\,\mathrm{Im}(\rho)\in[U,U+1]\}$. The basic local zero-count, used to convert 'a nearby zero exists' into a counting statement.

lem:4.1.summary: Comparing zeta-sums on different lines: $|\sum_{M_1<m\le M_2}m^{-\sigma-it}|\le4M_2^\beta\max(\dots)$ for $\beta\ge0$ — partial summation transferring a Dirichlet-sum bound from one vertical line to another. The technical glue between lem:2.1 and the $\zeta$-side estimates.

lem:6.1.summary: A large value of $\zeta'/\zeta(s)$ implies a nearby zero — the logarithmic-derivative analogue of lem:2.3, used in the reverse implication (large zeta values back to large Dirichlet-polynomial values, §7).

prop:9.1.summary: Conditional on conjecture:1.4: products $F(s)=\prod_{j=1}^kM_j(s)$ of Dirichlet polynomials have large-value sets of the size needed for the short-intervals argument. The estimate that, combined with a sieve/Perron argument, yields thm:1.5.

edges.note: A large value of $D$ is shown to force many large values of an associated zeta-sum.
Each large value of $\zeta$ produces a nearby zero, which the assumed zero-density bound then counts.
The iterated dichotomy gives the final measure bound on the large-$\zeta$-value set.
Works one well-separated class of zeros at a time.
The 'controlled by a zero-count' branch uses the measure-vs-zero-count estimate.
Picks the line on which to run the comparison.
Uses the local zero-count to turn 'a nearby zero' into a quantitative bound.
Approximate functional equation expresses $\zeta$ near the relevant line by a short sum.
Convexity bound controls the off-region contribution.
Transfers the Dirichlet-polynomial bound to the zeta-sum line via partial summation.
The almost-all short-intervals result follows from the large-value bound for products of Dirichlet polynomials plus a standard Perron/sieve argument.
Conditional on the stronger density hypothesis.
Uses the assumed power-saving zero-density bound.
Applies the zero-density ⟹ large-values implication to each factor $M_j$.
The reverse direction (large $\zeta$-values to large $D$-values) uses the $\zeta'/\zeta$ analogue.

main_results.description: Zero density ⟹ large value estimates, and (conditional on a power-saving density hypothesis) almost-all primes in $(\log X)^{2+\epsilon}$ intervals.

large_values_to_zeros.description: The chain large $D$-values ⟶ large zeta-sum values ⟶ large $\zeta(s)$ ⟶ nearby zeros, with the iterated dichotomy that bounds the large-zeta-value set.

zeta_tools.description: Approximate functional equation, the convexity bound, local zero-counting, and partial-summation transfer between lines.

short_intervals_application.description: Large values of products of Dirichlet polynomials, fed into a Perron/sieve argument.

HeathBrown:1979.title: Zero-density estimates for the Riemann zeta-function and Dirichlet L-functions

GuthMaynard.title: New large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials
